elements.sent-2411-001: Given a graph $G=(V,E)$, a function $f:V\to \{0,1,2\}$ is said to be a Roman Dominating function (RDF) if for every $v\in V$ with $f(v)=0$, there exists a vertex $u\in N(v)$ such that $f(u)=2$.
elements.sent-2411-002: A Roman Dominating function $f$ is said to be an Independent Roman Dominating function (IRDF), if $V_1\cup V_2$ forms an independent set, where $V_i=\{v\in V~\vert~f(v)=i\}$, for $i\in \{0,1,2\}$.
elements.sent-2411-003: The total weight of $f$ is equal to $\sum_{v\in V f(v)$, and is denoted as $w(f)$.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Independent Roman Domination Number) of $G$, denoted by $\gamma_R(G)$ (resp.
elements.sent-2411-006: For a given graph $G$, the problem of computing $\gamma_R(G)$ (resp.
elements.sent-2411-008: In this paper, we examine structural parameterizations of the (Independent) Roman Domination problem.
elements.sent-2411-009: We propose fixed-parameter tractable (FPT) algorithms for the (Independent) Roman Domination problem in graphs that are $k$ vertices away from a cluster graph.
elements.sent-2411-010: These graphs have a set of $k$ vertices whose removal results in a cluster graph.
elements.sent-2411-011: We refer to $k$ as the distance to the cluster graph.
elements.sent-2411-012: Specifically, we prove the following results when parameterized by the deletion distance $k$ to cluster graphs: we can find the Roman Domination Number (and Independent Roman Domination Number) in time $4^kn^{O(1)}$.
elements.sent-2411-013: In terms of lower bounds, we show that the Roman Domination number can not be computed in time $2^{\epsilon k n^{O(1)}$, for any $0<\epsilon <1$ unless a well-known conjecture, SETH fails.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In addition, we also show that the Roman Domination problem parameterized by distance to cluster, does not admit a polynomial kernel unless NP $\subseteq$ coNP$/$poly.
elements.sent-2411-015: The concept of Roman Dominating function originated in an article by Ian Stewart, titled ``Defend the Roman Empire!$"$ \cite{stewart1999defend , published in Scientific American.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Given a graph, where every vertex corresponds to a distinct geographical region within the historical narrative of the Roman Empire, the characterization of a location as secured or unsecured is delineated by the Roman Dominating function, denoted as $f$.
elements.sent-2411-017: Specifically, a vertex $v$ is said to be unsecured if it lacks stationed legions, expressed as $f(v) = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-018: Conversely, a secured location is one where one or two legions are stationed, denoted by $f(v) \in \{1, 2\}$.
elements.sent-2411-019: The strategic methodology for securing an unsecured area involves the deployment of a legion from a neighboring location.
elements.sent-2411-020: In the fourth century A.D., Emperor Constantine the Great enacted an edict precluding the transfer of a legion from a fortified position to an unfortified one if such an action would result in leaving the latter unsecured.
elements.sent-2411-021: Therefore, it is necessary to first have two legions at a given location ($f(v) = 2$) before sending one legion to a neighbouring location.
elements.sent-2411-022: This strategic approach, pioneered by Emperor Constantine the Great, effectively fortified the Roman Empire.
elements.sent-2411-023: Considering the substantial costs associated with legion deployment in specific areas, the Emperor aimed to strategically minimize the number of legions required to safeguard the Roman Empire.
elements.sent-2411-024: The notion of Roman Domination in graphs was first introduced in an article by Ian Stewart \cite{stewart1999defend .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Given a graph $G=(V,E)$, a Roman Dominating function (RDF) is defined as a function $f: V \to \{0,1,2\}$, where every vertex $v$, for which $f(v)=0$ must be adjacent to at least one vertex $u$ with $f(u)=2$.
elements.sent-2411-026: The weight of an RDF is defined as $w(f)=\sum_{v\in V f(v)$.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The Roman Domination Number is defined as $\gamma_R(G)= \min\{w(f)~\vert~f$ is an RDF of $G\}$.
elements.sent-2411-028: While the context is clear, if $f(v)=i$ for some RDF $f$, then we say that $v$ has label $i$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Given a graph $G=(V,E)$, a set $S\subseteq V$ is defined as independent set if any two vertices of $S$ are non-adjacent.
elements.sent-2411-030: A function $f$ is referred to as an Independent Roman Dominating function (IRDF) if $f$ is an RDF and $V_1\cup V_2$ is an independent set.
elements.sent-2411-031: The Independent Roman Domination Number is defined as $i_R(G)= \min\{w(f)~\vert~f$ is an IRDF of $G\}$.
elements.sent-2411-032: An IRDF $f$ of $G$ with $w(f)=i_R(G)$ is denoted as an $i_R(G)$-function of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-033: Given a graph $G=(V,E)$, the problem of computing $i_R(G)$ is known as Independent Roman Domination problem.
elements.sent-2411-034: One of the objectives of parameterized complexity is to identify parameters that render NP-hard problems fixed-parameter tractable (FPT).
elements.sent-2411-035: This is of practical significance because there are often small parameters, aside from solution size, that capture important practical inputs.
elements.sent-2411-036: Hence, it only makes sense to explore problems under a multitude of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-037: There has recently been a lot of research in this area.
elements.sent-2411-038: A key research direction involves identifying a parameter as small as possible, under which a problem becomes fixed-parameter tractable or admits a polynomial-sized kernel.
elements.sent-2411-039: Structural parameterization involves a parameter that is a function of the input structure rather than the standard output size.
elements.sent-2411-040: A recent trend in structural parameterization is to study problems parameterized by the deletion distance to various graph classes where the problem is efficiently solvable.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our parameter of interest is the `distance' of the graph from a natural class of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-042: Here, `distance' refers to the number of vertices that must be deleted from the graph to belong to the specified class.
elements.sent-2411-043: This article focuses on one such special class of graphs: cluster graphs, where each connected component of the graph is a clique.
elements.sent-2411-044: Note that both Roman Domination and Independent Roman Domination problems can be easily solved in cluster graphs.
elements.sent-2411-045: Given a graph $G=(V,E)$, the minimum number of vertices that need to be deleted from the graph so that the remaining graph becomes a cluster graph is called distance to cluster of $G$ (denoted by CVD size of $G$).
elements.sent-2411-046: This paper only considers simple, undirected, finite and nontrivial graphs.
elements.sent-2411-048: The number of neighbors of a vertex $v\in V$ defines its degree, which is represented by the symbol $deg(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-049: The maximum degree of the graph will be denoted by $\Delta$.
elements.sent-2411-050: For a set $U\subseteq V$, the notation $deg_{U (v)$ is used to represent the number of neighbors that a vertex $v$ has within the subset $U$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Additionally, we use $N_{U (v)$ to refer to the set of neighbors of vertex $v$ within $U$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Given a set $S\subseteq V$, $G\setminus S$ is defined as the graph induced on $V\setminus S$, that is $G[V\setminus S]$.
elements.sent-2411-053: A vertex of degree one is known as a pendant vertex.
elements.sent-2411-054: A set $S\subseteq V$ is said to be a dominating set if every vertex of $V\setminus S$ is adjacent to some vertex of $S$.
elements.sent-2411-055: A graph $G$ is said to be a complete graph if any two vertices of $G$ are adjacent.
elements.sent-2411-056: A set $S\subseteq V$ is said to be a clique if the subgraph of $G$ induced on $S$ is a complete graph.
elements.sent-2411-057: A graph is said to be a cluster graph if every component of the graph is a clique.
elements.sent-2411-058: For every positive integer $n$, $[n]$ denotes the set $\{1,2,\ldots,n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-059: Given a graph $G=(V,E)$ and a function $f:V\to \{0,1,2\}$, $f_H:V(H)\to \{0,1,2\}$ is defined to be the function $f$ restricted on $H$, where $H$ is an induced subgraph of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-060: For a graph $G = (V, E)$ and a function $f: V \to \{0,1,2\}$; we define $V_i = \{v\in V~\vert~f(v)=i\}$ for $i\in \{0,1,2\}$.
elements.sent-2411-061: The partition $(V_0,V_1,V_2)$ is said to be ordered partition of $V$ induced by $f$.
elements.sent-2411-062: Note that the function $f:V\to \{0,1,2\}$ and the ordered partition $(V_0,V_1,V_2)$ of $V$ have a one-to-one correspondence.
elements.sent-2411-063: So, when the context is clear, we write $f = (V_0, V_1, V_2)$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Given an RDF $f = (V_0,V_1,V_2)$, $(V_1,V_2)$ is said to be Roman Dominating pair corresponding to $f$.
elements.sent-2411-065: When the context is clear, we write Roman Dominating pair (omitting the notion of $f$).

elements.sent-2411-076: Question: Does there exists a subset $U'\subseteq U$, such that $U'\cap S_i\neq \emptyset$, for every $i\in [m]$?.
elements.sent-2411-077: We call the algorithm $\mathcal{B}$ as fixed parameter tractable algorithm (or FPT algorithm).

elements.sent-2411-082: It is believed that this subset relation is strict~\cite{DBLP:books/sp/CyganFKLMPPS15 .}
elements.sent-2411-083: Hence, a parameterized language that is hard for some complexity class above FPT is unlikely to be FPT.

elements.sent-2411-085: If a parameterized language $L \subseteq \Sigma^* \times \mathbb{N}$ can be solved by an algorithm running in time $\mathcal{O}\left(n^{f(k)}\right)$, then we say $L \in$ XP.

elements.sent-2411-093: It is well-known that a decidable parameterized problem is FPT if and only if it has a kernel.

elements.sent-2411-096: Towards that, we define the notion of polynomial parameter transformation.
elements.sent-2411-097: We say that $P_1$ is polynomial parameter reducible to $P_2$ if there exists a polynomial time computable function (or algorithm) $f: \Sigma^* \times \mathbb{N \rightarrow \Sigma^* \times \mathbb{N}$, a polynomial $p: \mathbb{N \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ such that $(x, k) \in P_1$ if and only if $f(x, k) \in P_2$ and $k^{\prime \leq p(k)$ where $f(x, k)=\left(x^{\prime , k^{\prime}\right)$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-104: From the parameterized complexity point of view, it is surprising that there does not exist much literature on the Roman Domination problem (except \cite{DBLP:conf/walcom/MohanapriyaRS23,DBLP:journals/ijcm/Fernau08 ), while the classical dominating set problem is very well studied.}

elements.section-2411-003: Variants of Roman Domination parameterized by CVD size
elements.sent-2411-118: In this section, we assume that a cluster vertex deletion set $S$ of size $k$ is given with the input graph $G=(V,E)$.
elements.sent-2411-119: If not, the algorithm mentioned in \cite{DBLP:journals/mst/BoralCKP16 can be used, which runs in $1.92^kn^{O(1)}$ time and outputs a cluster vertex deletion set of size at most $k$ or concludes that there does not any cluster vertex deletion set of size at most $k$.}
elements.sent-2411-120: In this section, we propose an FPT algorithm for the Roman Domination problem when the parameter is CVD size.
elements.sent-2411-121: We consider a CVD set $S$ as a part of the input, where $\vert S\vert=k$.
elements.sent-2411-122: Our algorithm starts with making a guess for $S_1=V_1\cap S$ and $S_2=V_2\cap S$, where $(V_1, V_2)$ is an optimal Roman Dominating pair.
elements.sent-2411-123: At first, a guess of $S_2$ is made from $S$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Then, the vertices of $N[S_2]\cap S$ are deleted from the graph.
elements.sent-2411-125: Then we guess $S_1$ from the remaining $S$ and delete $S_1$ from $S$.
elements.sent-2411-126: Note that $S$ is a CVD set, hence $G\setminus S$ is disjoint union of cliques.
elements.sent-2411-127: Let $G\setminus S= C_1\cup C_2\cup\ldots C_q$, where every $C_i$ is a clique and $\vert C_i\vert=\ell_i$, for $i\in [q]$, Note that $q\leq n-k$.
elements.sent-2411-128: After the selection of $S_1$ and $S_2$, every clique belongs to exactly one of the following three types: Type $0$ ($T_0$) cliques: $C_i$ is a $T_0$ clique if every vertex of $C_i$ is adjacent to at least one vertex of $S_2$.
elements.sent-2411-129: Type $1$ ($T_1$) cliques: $C_i$ is a $T_1$ clique if exactly one vertex of $C_i$ is not adjacent to any vertex of $S_2$.
elements.sent-2411-130: Type $2$ ($T_2$) cliques: $C_i$ is a $T_2$ clique if $C_i$ contains at least two vertices which are not adjacent to any vertex of $S_2$.
elements.sent-2411-131: We define an order $\rho_i$ on the vertices of the clique $C_i$ as follows: if $C_i$ is a $T_0$ or $T_2$ clique, then we order them arbitrarily; if $C_i$ is a $T_1$ clique, then the set $C_i\setminus N[S_2]$ contains exactly one vertex.
elements.sent-2411-132: We make that vertex the first vertex of $\rho_i$ and order the rest of the vertices of $C_i$ arbitrarily.
elements.sent-2411-133: Now we define an order $\rho$ on the vertex set of $G\setminus S$ as follows: $\rho=v_1,v_2,\ldots,v_{\vert G\setminus S\vert}$, where first $\ell_1$ vertices of $\rho$ are vertices of $C_1$ and follows the order $\rho_1$, then the next $\ell_2$ vertices of $\rho$ are vertices of $C_2$ and follows the order $\rho_2$ and so on.
elements.sent-2411-135: A Roman Dominating pair $(V'_1,V'_2)$ can be extended from $(S_1,S_2)$, which has same or less weight than $(V_1,V_2)$ and $V'_2\cap C_i\neq \emptyset$.
elements.sent-2411-136: As $C_i$ is a $T_2$ clique, let us assume that $v_1$ and $v_2$ are two vertices of $C_i$ which are not adjacent to $S_2$.
elements.sent-2411-137: If either of $v_1, v_2$ belongs to $V_2$, then we are done.
elements.sent-2411-138: If not, then two cases may arise: Case 1: At least one vertex among $v_1,v_2$ has label $0$.
elements.sent-2411-139: Without loss of generality, let $v_1$ has label $0$.
elements.sent-2411-140: Then, one neighbour $v$ of $v_1$ must have label $2$.
elements.sent-2411-141: But $v$ can not belong to $S$ as $V_2\cap S=S_2$ and $v_1$ has no neighbour in $S_2$.
elements.sent-2411-142: Hence, $v$ must belong to $C_i$, which implies that $C_i\cap V_2\neq \emptyset$.
elements.sent-2411-143: Case 2: None of $v_1,v_2$ has label $0$, which implies that both have label $1$.
elements.sent-2411-144: Now we construct a dominating pair $(V'_1,V'_2)$ as follows: $V'_2=V_2\cup\{v_1\}$ and $V'_1=V_1\setminus\{v_1,v_2\}$.
elements.sent-2411-145: Note that $(V'_1,V'_2)$ is a Roman Dominating pair and $(V'_1,V'_2)$ has the same weight as $(V_1,V_2)$ and $V'_2\cap C_i\neq \emptyset$.

elements.sent-2411-247: In this section, we propose a lower bound on the time-complexity of the RD-CVD problem.
elements.sent-2411-248: We also show that the RD-CVD (resp.

elements.sent-2411-253: There exists a polynomial time algorithm that takes an instance $(U,F,t)$ of the $d$-HITTING SET problem and outputs an instance $(G,2t)$ of the RD-CVD problem (and the RD-VC problem), where $G$ has a cluster vertex deletion set (and vertex cover) of size $\vert U\vert$; and $(U,F)$ has a $d$-hitting set of size at most $t$ if and only if $G$ has a Roman Dominating function of size at most $2t$.

elements.sent-2411-285: We state another theorem to show that the RD-CVD (and RD-VC) problem is unlikely to admit a polynomial kernel.
elements.sent-2411-286: Hence combining the above discussion with Theorem \ref{th:LB4 , the following theorem can be concluded.}
elements.sent-2411-287: The RD-CVD (and RD-VC) problem does not admit a polynomial kernel unless NP $\subseteq$ coNP$/$poly.
elements.sent-2411-288: By Theorem \ref{th:LB4 , the $d$-HITTING SET problem parameterized by universe size does not admit a polynomial kernel unless NP $\subseteq$ coNP$/$poly.}
elements.sent-2411-289: Since the reduction provided in Theorem \ref{th:LB2 is a polynomial parameter transformation (PPT), by Theorem \ref{th:kernel_hard , the RD-CVD and RD-VC do not admit a polynomial kernel unless NP $\subseteq$ coNP$/$poly.}}
